content.text: Langfuse is an open-source LLM engineering platform that provides tracing, prompt management, evaluation, and datasets in a single product. Unlike general-purpose APM tools, every concept in Langfuse is designed for the shape of LLM workloads: a Trace corresponds to one agent turn, a Generation records an LLM call with token counts and cost, a Score attaches a numeric or categorical evaluation result to any trace, and a Prompt is a versioned template fetched from the server at runtime rather than hardcoded in your repo. You can run Langfuse against their managed cloud or self-host with Docker Compose in under five minutes.

attrs.alt: Langfuse architecture showing agent code, SDK, server, storage and web UI

content.text: Install the Langfuse Python SDK and set credentials via environment variables. The SDK batches events and flushes them asynchronously so your agent code adds negligible latency.

pip install langfuse anthropic

# Cloud
export LANGFUSE_PUBLIC_KEY="pk-lf-..."
export LANGFUSE_SECRET_KEY="sk-lf-..."
export LANGFUSE_HOST="https://cloud.langfuse.com"  # default

# Self-hosted (Docker Compose)
export LANGFUSE_HOST="http://localhost:3000"

from langfuse import Langfuse

lf = Langfuse()  # reads env vars automatically

# Verify connection
lf.auth_check()  # raises if credentials are wrong

The simplest way to trace an agent is to decorate the entry function with @observe(). Langfuse automatically creates a Trace for each call and records the function name, arguments, return value, and wall-clock time. Nested @observe calls create child Spans inside the same trace without any explicit context passing.

from langfuse.decorators import observe, langfuse_context
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


@observe()  # creates a Trace
def run_agent(user_message: str) -> str:
    plan = call_model(user_message)
    return plan


@observe(as_type="generation")  # recorded as a Generation (token-aware)
def call_model(prompt: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    # Tell Langfuse about token usage
    langfuse_context.update_current_observation(
        input=prompt,
        output=resp.content[0].text,
        usage={
            "input": resp.usage.input_tokens,
            "output": resp.usage.output_tokens,
        },
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


result = run_agent("What is the boiling point of ethanol?")
print(result)
# Both spans appear in the Langfuse UI under one trace.

When the decorator approach does not fit — for example inside an existing async framework or when you need to attach metadata the decorator cannot infer — use the low-level Langfuse client directly. The key rule is that every observation (span, generation, event) must finish before you flush; otherwise the SDK may drop it.

from langfuse import Langfuse
import anthropic, uuid

lf = Langfuse()
client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def run_agent_manual(user_message: str, session_id: str) -> str:
    # Root trace — one per agent turn
    trace = lf.trace(
        name="agent_turn",
        session_id=session_id,
        user_id="user-123",
        input={"message": user_message},
        tags=["production"],
    )

    # Child span for a retrieval step
    retrieval_span = trace.span(name="retrieval", input={"query": user_message})
    docs = fake_retrieval(user_message)
    retrieval_span.end(output={"doc_count": len(docs)})

    # Generation for the LLM call
    gen = trace.generation(
        name="llm_call",
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        input=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1024,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
    )
    answer = resp.content[0].text
    gen.end(
        output=answer,
        usage={
            "input": resp.usage.input_tokens,
            "output": resp.usage.output_tokens,
            "unit": "TOKENS",
        },
    )

    trace.update(output={"answer": answer})
    lf.flush()  # send buffered events before returning
    return answer


def fake_retrieval(query: str) -> list[str]:
    return [f"doc about {query}"]

A Score links a numeric or categorical value to a trace or generation. You can add scores at collection time (e.g. thumbs-up feedback from a user) or offline via a separate evaluation pipeline that re-reads traces and scores them with an LLM judge. Both paths use the same score() method.

# Inline user feedback — call this after you get a rating from the UI
def record_user_feedback(trace_id: str, thumbs_up: bool) -> None:
    lf.score(
        trace_id=trace_id,
        name="user_feedback",
        value=1.0 if thumbs_up else 0.0,
        comment="collected from thumbs widget",
    )


# Offline LLM-as-judge evaluation
def score_faithfulness(trace_id: str, generation_id: str, answer: str, context: str) -> None:
    judge_prompt = (
        f"Context: {context}\n\nAnswer: {answer}\n\n"
        "Rate how faithful this answer is to the context. "
        "Respond with only a number between 0 and 1."
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": judge_prompt}],
    )
    try:
        score_value = float(resp.content[0].text.strip())
    except ValueError:
        return
    lf.score(
        trace_id=trace_id,
        observation_id=generation_id,  # attach to specific generation
        name="faithfulness",
        value=score_value,
    )


# Categorical score
def record_category(trace_id: str, category: str) -> None:
    lf.score(
        trace_id=trace_id,
        name="response_type",
        value=1.0,
        string_value=category,  # "factual", "creative", "refusal"
        data_type="CATEGORICAL",
    )

Langfuse Prompt Management lets you store, version, and deploy prompt templates from the UI without a code change. At runtime the SDK fetches the active version from the server (with a local cache so latency is near-zero after the first call). If you link a generation to a prompt version, Langfuse tracks which version produced which trace.

from langfuse import Langfuse

lf = Langfuse()

# Fetch the production version of a prompt (cached locally for 60s)
prompt = lf.get_prompt("support-agent-system")

# Compile with variables defined in the template
compiled = prompt.compile(product_name="Acme Widget", tier="pro")
print(compiled)  # → the filled-in system prompt string

# Link the generation to the prompt so dashboards show version → score
gen = trace.generation(
    name="llm_call",
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    prompt=prompt,          # tracks prompt name + version + config
    input=[{"role": "system", "content": compiled},
           {"role": "user", "content": user_message}],
)

# Fetch a specific version for A/B testing
prompt_v2 = lf.get_prompt("support-agent-system", version=2)
prompt_prod = lf.get_prompt("support-agent-system", label="production")

A Langfuse Dataset is a named collection of DatasetItems, each holding an input and an optional expected_output. You run your agent against each item, record the actual output as a trace linked to the item, then score that trace. The dashboard shows score distributions per dataset run, making before-and-after prompt comparisons straightforward.

from langfuse import Langfuse

lf = Langfuse()

# Create a dataset (idempotent)
lf.create_dataset(name="support-qa", description="Support agent golden set")

# Populate it with items
for item in [
    {"input": "How do I reset my password?", "expected": "Use the reset link..."},
    {"input": "Where is my order #1234?", "expected": "Check the tracking..."},
]:
    lf.create_dataset_item(
        dataset_name="support-qa",
        input=item["input"],
        expected_output=item["expected"],
    )


# Run the agent against each item and link traces
def run_dataset_eval(agent_fn, dataset_name: str, run_name: str) -> None:
    dataset = lf.get_dataset(dataset_name)
    for item in dataset.items:
        with item.observe(run_name=run_name) as trace_id:
            # item.observe() creates a trace linked to this item + run
            output = agent_fn(item.input)
            # Score immediately if we have a reference
            if item.expected_output:
                is_match = item.expected_output.lower() in output.lower()
                lf.score(
                    trace_id=trace_id,
                    name="string_match",
                    value=1.0 if is_match else 0.0,
                )


run_dataset_eval(run_agent, "support-qa", run_name="haiku-v2-system-prompt")

The Langfuse client exposes a query API so you can fetch traces in Python rather than clicking through the UI. This is useful for batch scoring pipelines that pull recent unscored traces and pass them through an LLM judge.

from langfuse import Langfuse
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

lf = Langfuse()

# Fetch traces from the last hour with no score on 'faithfulness'
trace_page = lf.fetch_traces(
    from_timestamp=datetime.utcnow() - timedelta(hours=1),
    tags=["production"],
    page=1,
    limit=20,
)

for trace in trace_page.data:
    has_faithfulness = any(s.name == "faithfulness" for s in trace.scores)
    if has_faithfulness:
        continue
    # Pull full trace with generations
    full = lf.fetch_trace(trace.id)
    for obs in full.observations:
        if obs.type == "GENERATION" and obs.output:
            score_faithfulness(
                trace_id=trace.id,
                generation_id=obs.id,
                answer=obs.output,
                context="",  # enrich from your retrieval store
            )

lf.flush()
Free tier; paid by trace volume

Forgetting lf.flush() — the SDK buffers events and sends them on a background thread. If your process exits (e.g. a CLI script or a Lambda) before the buffer drains, traces are lost. Always call lf.flush() or lf.shutdown() at the end of short-lived processes.

Logging full message content in production — storing the entire conversation in trace input/output can violate data retention policies and bloat storage. Log only the fields you will actually query: token counts, trace IDs, error flags.

Not linking generations to prompts — if you forget to pass prompt= to trace.generation(), Langfuse cannot show you which prompt version produced which score distribution. This makes A/B testing opaque.

Scoring inside the hot path — running an LLM judge synchronously before returning the agent's answer adds hundreds of milliseconds. Run scoring as a background job that reads traces from the Langfuse query API after the user has already received their response.

Every trace accepts tags (a list of strings) and metadata (an arbitrary dict). Tags let you filter in the Langfuse UI by deployment environment, agent version, or experiment name. Metadata carries richer data — the Git SHA, the model version, or a request ID — that you want to search against without creating a formal dimension.

import os

GIT_SHA = os.getenv("GIT_SHA", "local")
ENV = os.getenv("DEPLOY_ENV", "development")


def run_agent_with_tags(user_message: str, user_id: str) -> str:
    trace = lf.trace(
        name="agent_turn",
        user_id=user_id,
        session_id=f"session-{user_id}",
        tags=[ENV, "support", f"sha:{GIT_SHA}"],
        metadata={
            "agent_version": "2.4.1",
            "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
            "prompt_name": "support-agent-system",
            "request_id": os.urandom(8).hex(),
        },
        input={"message": user_message},
    )

    answer = _run_inner(trace, user_message)
    trace.update(output={"answer": answer})
    lf.flush()
    return answer


# In the Langfuse UI you can now filter:
#   tag = "production"  AND  metadata.agent_version = "2.4.1"
# to isolate traces for a specific rollout.

Langfuse datasets make it straightforward to add a regression gate to CI: run the agent against the golden set, score every output, aggregate the scores, and fail the build if the mean drops below a threshold. The run_name labels each CI run by Git SHA so you can compare score trends across commits in the Langfuse UI.

import sys

DATASET_NAME = "support-qa"
SCORE_NAME = "string_match"
MIN_SCORE = 0.75  # fail CI if mean drops below 75%


def ci_eval(agent_fn, run_name: str) -> None:
    dataset = lf.get_dataset(DATASET_NAME)
    scores: list[float] = []

    for item in dataset.items:
        with item.observe(run_name=run_name) as trace_id:
            output = agent_fn(item.input)
            if item.expected_output:
                match = item.expected_output.lower() in output.lower()
                score_val = 1.0 if match else 0.0
                lf.score(trace_id=trace_id, name=SCORE_NAME, value=score_val)
                scores.append(score_val)

    lf.flush()
    if not scores:
        print("No scored items — check dataset")
        sys.exit(1)

    mean = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    print(f"{run_name}: {SCORE_NAME} = {mean:.2%} ({len(scores)} items)")
    if mean < MIN_SCORE:
        print(f"FAIL: score {mean:.2%} < threshold {MIN_SCORE:.2%}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("PASS")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import os
    ci_eval(run_agent, run_name=f"ci-{os.getenv('GIT_SHA', 'local')}")

Langfuse gives agents a single platform for tracing (Trace + Generation + Span), evaluation (Score), prompt versioning (Prompt), and golden-set testing (Dataset). Use @observe() for decorator-based tracing that needs zero boilerplate, the manual trace/span/generation API when you need full control over metadata, score() to attach inline user feedback or offline LLM-judge results, and fetch_traces() to build batch evaluation pipelines that pull and re-score production traffic. For regulated workloads, deploy the MIT-licensed Docker image on your own infrastructure and point LANGFUSE_HOST at it.
